claim: ICER assessed lecanemab (Leqembi) for early Alzheimer's disease; independent panel voted 12-3 that evidence is not adequate to demonstrate net health benefit over supportive care, and unanimously (15-0) rated long-term value as 'low' at current $26,500/year list price.
verdict: not adequate net health benefit; low long-term value
source: ICER Final Evidence Report, California Technology Assessment Forum
quote: Lecanemab has been approved by the FDA and has a list price of $26,500 per year. ICER has calculated a health-benefit price benchmark (HBPB) for lecanemab to be between $8,900 – $21,500 per year.
reason: Panel found evidence promising but inconclusive; brain swelling/bleeding risks create uncertainty about net benefit at current pricing.
precedent_implication: Anti-amyloid mAbs face payer resistance unless priced at a steep discount to list; a CNS program relying on amyloid-reduction surrogate must expect coverage-with-evidence-development or restricted coverage.

claim: ICER assessed nusinersen (Spinraza) and onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma) for spinal muscular atrophy; Council voted unanimously that Spinraza at $750,000/year represents 'low' long-term value; Zolgensma price-range for cost-effectiveness estimated at $310,000–$900,000.
verdict: low long-term value for Spinraza at current price; Zolgensma price threshold $310K-$900K
price_or_conditions: Spinraza list price $750,000 first year / $375,000 annually; cost-effective range $72,000-$130,000 (yr1)
source: ICER Final Evidence Report on Treatments for SMA
quote: To reach commonly cited cost-effectiveness thresholds of $100,000-$150,000 per QALY gained, Zolgensma's price for Type I SMA would need to be between $310,000-$900,000 per treatment.
reason: High list price far exceeds ICER thresholds; no outcomes-based contract in place at assessment time.
precedent_implication: Gene therapy and ASO drugs targeting rare neurological diseases face severe 'low value' verdicts unless outcomes-based risk-sharing contracts are pre-negotiated with payers; absence of such contracts is a reimbursement risk signal.

claim: CMS issued a final National Coverage Determination (NCD 200.3) in April 2022 covering FDA-approved anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies for Alzheimer's disease under Coverage with Evidence Development (CED), requiring enrollment in CMS-approved clinical trials for non-traditionally-approved drugs.
indication: Alzheimer's disease (mild cognitive impairment, mild dementia)
verdict: covered under CED; traditional FDA approval broadens coverage; accelerated approval restricted to RCT only
price_or_conditions: CED enrollment required; PET scan may be covered if required by trial protocol
quote: Medicare will cover monoclonal antibodies that target amyloid (or plaque) for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease that receive traditional approval from the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) under coverage with evidence development.
reason: Insufficient evidence of improved health outcomes to support broad Medicare coverage; CED allows data collection in real-world practice.
precedent_implication: Any CNS drug class where surrogate endpoints drive FDA approval should anticipate CMS CED requirement; failure to enroll in CMS-approved registries blocks Medicare reimbursement.

claim: CMS expanded lecanemab (Leqembi) Medicare coverage in July 2023 following traditional FDA approval, covering under Coverage with Evidence Development via CMS-approved registries, with patients paying 20% coinsurance after Part B deductible.
verdict: covered under CED in CMS-approved registries; not unrestricted coverage
price_or_conditions: 20% Part B coinsurance; CED registry enrollment required; physician must participate in qualifying registry
cms_coverage: NCD 200.3 expanded after traditional FDA approval July 2023
source: CMS Statement on broader Medicare coverage of Leqembi, July 2023
quote: Consistent with CMS' National Coverage Determination, Medicare is covering this drug when a physician and clinical team participates in the collection of evidence about how these drugs work in the real world, also known as a registry.
reason: Traditional FDA approval triggered expanded CED pathway under NCD 200.3; CMS requires registry enrollment to gather real-world evidence.
precedent_implication: Traditional FDA approval is insufficient for full unrestricted Medicare reimbursement in CNS; CED via real-world registry remains the coverage condition, creating uptake friction and limiting addressable commercial market.

claim: ICER assessed buprenorphine extended-release and naltrexone extended-release for opioid use disorder; found extended-release formulations provide only marginal clinical benefits over generic alternatives at significantly higher prices.
verdict: marginal benefit over generics; cost-effectiveness thresholds not met at list prices
price_or_conditions: ER formulations priced far above generics; Probuphine needs 53-65% discount
source: ICER Evidence Report on Extended-Release Treatments for Opioid Use Disorder
quote: Even assuming a small clinical benefit with Probuphine, its current price exceeds commonly cited cost-effectiveness thresholds; its list price would need to be reduced by 53-65% to meet these thresholds.
reason: ER formulations improve adherence compliance but clinical outcomes data over generic comparators are limited; payers apply cost-minimization framework.
precedent_implication: CNS addiction/dependency drugs face 'cost-minimization' payer logic when generics exist; incremental-benefit claims require RCT data vs. generic comparator to justify premium pricing.
